story.iWant: To measure comprehension skills from each session transcript
story.soThat: Learning trajectory can be tracked across sessions for comprehension-led courses

assumptions: Student has been guided through a passage or text
Transcript contains student responses to comprehension questions
Tutor has asked open-ended questions about the text

acceptanceCriteria.title: Theme understanding scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student discussing themes from a passage
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their articulation of central themes
acceptanceCriteria.then: A theme understanding score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Theme understanding is the core comprehension skill — can they identify and express what the text is about?

acceptanceCriteria.title: Inference skill scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student answering questions about implied meaning
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their ability to draw conclusions beyond the literal text
acceptanceCriteria.then: An inference skill score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Inference separates surface reading from deep comprehension

acceptanceCriteria.title: Evidence usage scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student making claims about the text
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses whether claims are supported with textual references
acceptanceCriteria.then: An evidence usage score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Evidence usage shows analytical discipline — grounding interpretation in the text

acceptanceCriteria.title: Recall accuracy scored
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their recall of content from prior sessions
acceptanceCriteria.then: A recall accuracy score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Recall shows retention and consolidation of learning across sessions

constraints.description: COMP_RECALL should be scored as null or excluded from aggregation on session 1 (nothing to recall yet)

failureConditions.implication: Not enough student speech to assess comprehension — use neutral defaults

parameters.name: theme_understanding
parameters.description: Ability to articulate central themes in own words without parroting the text or tutor

subMetrics.name: Articulation Clarity
subMetrics.description: How clearly the student expresses the theme

definitions.high: Expresses theme in own words with clarity and confidence
definitions.low: Vague, confused, or unable to name a theme

subMetrics.name: Independence
subMetrics.description: Whether the student generates the theme independently vs parroting

definitions.high: Arrives at the theme through own reasoning
definitions.low: Repeats tutor's words or reads directly from text

subMetrics.name: Depth of Understanding
subMetrics.description: How far beyond surface meaning the student goes

definitions.high: Connects theme to wider ideas, symbolism, or personal insight
definitions.low: Identifies only the literal surface plot

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Cannot identify or articulate any theme

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Vague or parroted theme identification

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Identifies theme but struggles to articulate independently

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Articulates theme clearly in own words

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Deep thematic insight with connections beyond the text

scoringAnchors.example: Um... it's about... a girl? And a garden?
scoringAnchors.rationale: Cannot move beyond literal plot description, no thematic insight

scoringAnchors.example: I think it's about being lonely. The girl is lonely because her parents died.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies a valid theme (loneliness) but stays at surface level, close to plot summary

scoringAnchors.example: The story is about transformation — Mary starts as this spoiled, neglected child and the garden kind of changes her. Like she grows as a person while the garden grows.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies transformation theme, begins to connect character growth to symbolism, uses own words

scoringAnchors.example: I think the garden represents renewal — not just for Mary but for everyone who touches it. Colin's healing mirrors the garden's restoration. It's about how nurturing something outside yourself can heal what's broken inside.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Multi-layered thematic insight, connects symbolism across characters, independent analysis

scoringAnchors.example: What strikes me is how Burnett uses the garden as a counterpoint to the colonial setting Mary comes from — the neglected English garden versus the 'managed' world of India. The theme isn't just renewal, it's about what happens when you stop trying to control nature and let things grow wild. That applies to the children too — they thrive when freed from adult control.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Sophisticated multi-layered analysis, original insight connecting themes across contexts, critical perspective on the author's intent

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student has strong thematic understanding. Push deeper — ask about authorial intent, alternative interpretations, or how themes connect to other texts.
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student struggles with themes. Scaffold with closed questions first: 'What is this paragraph about?' before asking 'What's the bigger idea?'
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student can identify themes but needs support articulating them independently. Use prompts like 'Can you say that in your own words?' or 'What makes you think that?'

parameters.name: inference_skill
parameters.description: Ability to draw conclusions not explicitly stated in the text — reading between the lines

subMetrics.name: Beyond Literal
subMetrics.description: Goes beyond what is explicitly written

definitions.high: Identifies implied meaning, subtext, tone, and mood
definitions.low: Only restates what is directly written

subMetrics.name: Textual Grounding
subMetrics.description: Connects inferences back to specific text

definitions.high: Points to specific words, phrases, or passages that support the inference

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Only repeats what is explicitly written

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Attempts inference but stays close to literal text

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Draws some valid inferences but reasoning is incomplete

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Consistent inference with textual support

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Sophisticated inference connecting subtext, tone, and implication

scoringAnchors.example: It says she went to the garden. So she went to the garden.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Pure literal restatement, no inference at all

scoringAnchors.example: I think she was sad? Because it says she was alone.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Attempts inference (sad) but stays very close to literal (alone = sad), tentative

scoringAnchors.example: When it says 'she looked at the key as if it were a living thing', I think that means the garden is becoming really important to her — like it's giving her purpose.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Valid inference from figurative language, connects to character motivation, references specific text

scoringAnchors.example: The writer uses the word 'contrary' repeatedly — I don't think it's just describing Mary's behaviour. It's almost like the narrator is challenging us to look past our first impression of her. By calling her contrary so often, we start questioning whether she really is.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Infers authorial technique from word choice patterns, meta-level reading

scoringAnchors.example: The shift from passive to active voice when Mary starts working in the garden — 'things were done for her' becomes 'she dug and pulled' — mirrors her psychological transformation. Burnett is using grammar itself as a metaphor for agency.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Linguistic inference at structural level, connects form to meaning, highly sophisticated

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student infers well. Push toward evaluative inference: 'Why do you think the writer chose to do it this way?' or 'Could it mean something different?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student stays literal. Model inference explicitly: 'When it says X, it might suggest Y. What do you think?' Use the text to scaffold.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student attempts inference but needs grounding. Ask 'Where in the text does it suggest that?' to build evidence-linking habits.

parameters.name: evidence_usage
parameters.description: Ability to support claims and interpretations with specific references to the text

subMetrics.name: Specificity
subMetrics.description: How specific the textual references are

definitions.high: Quotes specific words, phrases, or page references
definitions.low: Vague or no references to the text

subMetrics.name: Relevance
subMetrics.description: Whether the evidence actually supports the claim

definitions.low: Evidence is tangential or disconnected from the claim

subMetrics.name: Integration
subMetrics.description: How well evidence is woven into the argument

definitions.high: Evidence is embedded naturally in the student's reasoning
definitions.low: Evidence is quoted without connection to the argument

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Makes no reference to the text

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Occasional vague references ('it says somewhere...')

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: References text but not always relevant or specific

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Consistently supports claims with specific, relevant evidence

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Seamlessly integrates evidence into sophisticated argument

scoringAnchors.example: I just think she's mean. I dunno why.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Assertion with zero textual support

scoringAnchors.example: She's mean because it says so in the story somewhere.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Acknowledges text exists but can't locate or quote it

scoringAnchors.example: The text describes her as 'sour-faced' which shows she's not very pleasant to be around.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Specific quote, relevant to claim, but analysis is thin

scoringAnchors.example: On page 3, the phrase 'as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived' is really harsh — it's not just saying she's difficult, it's comparing her to an animal. That suggests the author wants us to feel repelled by her at first.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Specific quote with page reference, analyses language technique, connects to authorial intent

scoringAnchors.example: Burnett builds our dislike of Mary through accumulation — 'disagreeable', 'tyrannical', 'selfish', 'pig' all in one sentence. But then notice how in chapter 4, the descriptors shift to 'curious' and 'awakened'. The evidence of her transformation isn't in what she does — it's in the words the narrator chooses to describe her.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Tracks evidence across passages, analyses patterns in authorial language, builds cumulative argument from multiple text references

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student uses evidence well. Challenge them with competing evidence: 'But what about when it says X? Does that change your view?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student doesn't reference text. Model it: 'Let's look at the exact words. Can you find where it says that?' Build the habit of going back to the text.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student quotes but doesn't analyse. Ask 'Why did you choose that quote?' or 'What specific words are doing the work in that sentence?'

parameters.name: recall_accuracy
parameters.description: Ability to remember and reference content from prior sessions — retention and consolidation

subMetrics.name: Content Accuracy
subMetrics.description: Whether recalled content is factually correct

definitions.high: Recalls content accurately without confusion or invention
definitions.low: Confuses details, invents content, or recalls nothing

definitions.high: Recalls specific details, quotes, character names, plot points
definitions.low: Only vague impressions ('it was about a girl')

subMetrics.name: Spontaneity
subMetrics.description: Whether recall is spontaneous or requires heavy prompting

definitions.high: Recalls without prompting or with minimal cues
definitions.low: Cannot recall even with specific prompts

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: No recall of prior session content

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Vague recall with significant prompting needed

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Recalls main ideas but misses details

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Accurate recall of themes and key details

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Detailed, spontaneous recall connecting across sessions

scoringAnchors.example: I don't remember. We talked about something... a book maybe?
scoringAnchors.rationale: Essentially no recall of prior session content

scoringAnchors.example: Oh yeah, we read about... a girl and a garden? I can't remember her name though.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Vague plot recall, no character names or theme recollection

scoringAnchors.example: Last time we talked about Mary and how she found the garden. We said the garden was like a symbol for something... growth maybe?
scoringAnchors.rationale: Recalls character name, basic plot, and begins to recall thematic discussion

scoringAnchors.example: We discussed how Mary transforms from this spoiled child into someone who cares about the garden and other people. You asked me about the word 'contrary' and I said I thought the narrator was being ironic.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Accurate recall of discussion content, specific vocabulary, and own contributions

scoringAnchors.example: In session 1 we covered the opening chapters — Mary's isolation in India and her neglect. In session 2 we looked at the garden discovery and I argued that the garden represents Mary's inner world. Today I want to revisit that because I've been thinking about whether Colin's garden experience supports or complicates that reading.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Detailed cross-session recall, recalls own interpretations, builds on prior learning, self-directed

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong recall. Build on it: 'You mentioned X last time — has your thinking changed?' Use their memory as a foundation for deeper work.
promptGuidance.whenLow: Weak recall. Use structured retrieval: 'Last time we talked about [topic]. Can you remember anything about that?' Provide cues rather than answers.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Partial recall. Use spaced retrieval technique: 'What was the main theme we identified?' If they struggle, narrow it: 'Was it about loneliness or transformation?'
